title: Novartis sharpening India strategy as a pure-play innovator
summary: Novartis India country president and managing director Amitabh Dube said India is gradually moving beyond its traditional role as a clinical development base and is beginning to participate in early scientific discovery and Phase I clinical research.
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
Synopsis

Novartis India country president and managing director Amitabh Dube said India is gradually moving beyond its traditional role as a clinical development base and is beginning to participate in early scientific discovery and Phase I clinical research.

Listen to this article in summarized format

Swiss pharmaceutical major Novartis is strengthening its positioning in India as a focused innovator in key therapy areas such as cardiovascular diseases, oncology and immunology after exiting its listed subsidiary earlier this year. The company is also deepening India’s role in its global innovation pipeline, with the country now contributing to almost every molecule commercialised worldwide by the drugmaker.

Novartis India country president and managing director Amitabh Dube said India is gradually moving beyond its traditional role as a clinical development base and is beginning to participate in early scientific discovery and Phase I clinical research.

In February, Novartis announced the sale of its 71% stake in its listed subsidiary, Novartis India, for nearly Rs 1,446 crore to a group of private equity investors led by ChrysCapital as part of a strategic review. Through the transaction, the company exited its legacy medicines portfolio while retaining its unlisted arm, Novartis Healthcare, which will continue to focus on innovative medicines in India.

Traditionally, multinational pharmaceutical companies have relied on India largely for Phase II and Phase III clinical trials, while early-stage research and innovation-led activities have remained concentrated in developed markets. However, Novartis has now initiated Phase I clinical research activities in Ahmedabad and plans to continue late-stage development programmes across its major therapeutic segments.

India has contributed to the development of several important global therapies from Novartis, including Coartem Baby, the first antimalarial treatment designed for infants, cholesterol-lowering cardiovascular therapy Inclisiran, and Lutetium-177 vipivotide tetraxetan, a targeted radioligand therapy for prostate cancer.

Novartis operates one of its three global development hubs in India, with centres in Hyderabad and Mumbai, alongside facilities in Basel, Switzerland, and the US. The India Development Hub played a role in multiple aspects of the Coartem Baby programme, including clinical operations, pharmaceutical development, regulatory submissions and safety monitoring.

Dube said the company’s broader strategy has been to evolve into a pure-play innovative medicines company. Retaining legacy brands without adequate investments and strategic focus would not have aligned with that vision, he said.

He added that Novartis continues to maintain a strong commitment to India, where it employs more than 9,000 people. The company launched two new molecules in the country last year and plans to introduce one more this year, followed by two additional launches next year.

( Originally published on May 11, 2026 )
